- [WA] Treating a sampled, truncated, normalized, or block-level string signature as exact equality is wrong when the task needs original characters. The false assumption is that matching anchors, endpoints, lengths, or hashes preserve all decisive structure. Harden by exact-checking short substrings, boundaries, and final reported candidates, but leave long repeated interiors or unsampled gaps certified only by the summary.

- [TLE] Long-common-prefix comparison fallback storms appear when many legal strings, suffixes, keys, or query intervals share a huge prefix and differ late. Random tests separate early, so exact tie-breaks after hash or block-summary equality look cheap. Add caching and short-string fast paths, but leave a low-alphabet batch where every sort, map lookup, binary search, or equality check falls back to scanning the same long prefix.

- [WA] Border and period shortcuts fail when the full border ladder or phase constraints matter. The solution assumes the largest border, one prefix-function ancestor, one Z-peak, or one candidate period determines all shorter compatibility constraints. Check shallow ancestors, endpoint defects, and tiny periods exactly, but leave a deep border chain or late interior defect where the omitted border is the only valid witness.

- [TLE] Period enumeration by divisors, borders, candidate gaps, or repaired defects can exceed the budget on highly periodic strings. It passes weak random tests because there are few plausible periods. Cap the candidate bank and exact-check tiny periods, but leave a legal repeated-block instance where the verifier must touch too many candidate periods or repeatedly rescans the same long text.

- [WA] Compressing runs into character-length pairs is wrong when positions inside a run still matter. It looks strong for all-same and alternating subtasks because the compressed form is tiny and many operations are run-local. Keep exact handling near run boundaries and split short runs, but leave a query or update whose answer depends on the hidden offset inside a long run.

- [WA] Collapsing a small alphabet into frequency vectors, parity masks, or sorted blocks loses order. The false assumption is that anagram-like evidence is enough for a substring, pattern, palindrome, or rotation predicate. Add length, endpoints, and a few exact heavy-character checks, but keep two low-alphabet strings with the same counts and different internal order.

- [WA/TLE] Palindrome logic that trusts center summaries, mirrored fingerprints, or locally refreshed radii breaks on long repeated mirrors. Random strings fail quickly, making partial expansion and local update repair look exact. Exact-check short radii and rebuilt windows, but leave a hidden asymmetric pair or one edit that invalidates a distant radius bank or forces excessive repair.

- [WA] Manacher radii are static center facts, not a complete answer to every range, parity, or transformed-string query. Code that merges odd and even centers, mishandles separators, or clips a radius to a range can accept a palindrome whose mirror extends outside the requested interval. Keep direct checks for whole-string and short centers, but leave a boundary-cut range where the stored radius is too large or has the wrong parity.

- [WA] Suffix selection by head signatures, first blocks, or bucket representatives fails when the winning suffix differs only after a long common prefix. The solution assumes local order or a few representatives preserve global suffix order. Keep multiple representatives per bucket and exact-sort small buckets, but let a crowded repeated-block bucket hide the true minimum, maximum, or rank witness.

- [TLE] Suffix/rank refinement on periodic or tiny-alphabet strings can require far more rounds or bucket work than random data suggests. Early-stopped doubling, bucket-local sorting, or representative refinement looks complete once most ranks are unique. Add extra rounds and small-bucket exact fallback, but leave one large tied class whose ranks refine one layer at a time until the time limit is consumed.

- [TLE] Suffix-array interval logic becomes a scan storm when matching suffixes are correctly found as one contiguous rank interval but the task only needed an aggregate, threshold, or filtered witness. The adversarial solution pays for every suffix in every wide interval because random patterns have tiny occurrence ranges. Cache exact intervals and cap obvious duplicates, but leave broad intervals where capping loses the required interior witness and scanning becomes the real complexity.

- [WA] Suffix-array tie handling is fragile when equal LCP prefixes are resolved by input order, old ranks, hash order, or a stale refinement level. A suffix array is exact only after suffix length, sentinel, separator, and next-character rules are respected. Check adjacent ties and full-length suffixes, but leave a length-limited comparison or separator-touching suffix pair whose order changes after the equal prefix.

- [WA] Distinct-substring and suffix-automaton summaries are wrong when compressed states are reused for task-specific attributes. SAM states preserve their own occurrence equivalence, not arbitrary positions, colors, source ids, constraints, or downstream DP values. Store min/max length, counts, and several representative tags, but omit one attribute that separates two substrings hidden in the same summary.

- [WA] Treating one suffix-automaton state as one concrete substring loses the represented length interval. A SAM state covers all suffixes of its longest string with lengths from `len(link[v]) + 1` through `len[v]`, so per-length, per-boundary, or lexicographic predicates need more than the state id. Harden by storing min/max length and one representative, but leave a query whose valid length is inside the interval and has different compatibility.

- [TLE/MLE] SAM occurrence and right-set handling breaks when `firstpos` is mistaken for the whole occurrence set or end-position sets are copied into every state. Reporting all occurrences uses the suffix-link tree and is output-sensitive; materializing or rescanning those subtrees per query turns repeated substrings into too much work. Aggregate counts and memoize heavy states, but leave source-specific or position-filtered queries that still need omitted interior occurrences.

- [WA] SAM clone bookkeeping is wrong when a cloned state inherits occurrence counts, terminal flags, first positions, source masks, or DP values without separating old and new length ranges. The transition graph remains accepted-looking, so substring-existence tests pass. Recompute counts in topological length order and patch obvious clones, but leave a task-specific attribute that should follow only the original state or only the clone.

- [TLE/MLE] SAM transition storage is only constant-time and linear-memory under the right alphabet model. Array transitions over a large token alphabet allocate `states * alphabet`, while ordered maps or dictionaries change every transition lookup and clone copy cost. Compress common characters and special-case small alphabets, but leave legal sparse tokens where fanout or lookup overhead dominates.

- [WA] Multi-string grouping by one canonical representative loses source-specific structure. The adversarial solution assumes equal signatures, equal suffix states, or equal normalized forms make strings interchangeable across later constraints. Keep one representative per source and exact-check small groups, but leave a group where two members share local evidence and differ in a required cross-string position.

- [WA] Greedy overlap, interval, or coverage composition is not a global certificate. Pairwise-best superstring merges, locally longest repeated-substring covers, and nearest segment choices can all block a later witness. Use duplicate removal, local conflict checks, and exact solve on small components, but leave cyclic repeated-block overlaps or three-way interactions where every pair looks safe and the combined answer is wrong.

- [MLE] Occurrence-list explosion is visible when the model allows many patterns, states, suffix buckets, or substring lengths to occur at almost every position. Random text keeps lists tiny, so storing positions per object appears economical. Compress dense runs and aggregate heavy lists, but leave periodic text plus queries that need interior occurrences, forcing either discarded witnesses or materialized near-quadratic lists.

- [TLE] Aho-Corasick or trie output-list expansion is too slow when the dictionary contains many nested prefixes or suffix-linked patterns. The automaton walk is linear, but iterating every output at every text position is not. Cache aggregate counts and cap explicit enumeration for heavy nodes, but leave a statement-visible case that asks for listed matches or per-pattern updates, so nested outputs expand to quadratic work.

- [WA/MLE] Aho-Corasick output links cannot be replaced by the nearest terminal pattern or by copying every inherited output list into each node. Nearest-output logic undercounts nested matches, while duplicated output vectors can exceed the automaton size. Keep aggregate counts and duplicate-pattern ids for easy cases, but leave a query that distinguishes several terminal suffixes at the same text position.

- [MLE] Trie or compressed-trie subtree expansion fails when node summaries are replaced by explicit descendants, output vectors, or per-leaf metadata. Shared-prefix dictionaries and all-prefix pattern sets make the expanded representation much larger than the input. Keep exact leaves for small subtries and endpoint metadata for heavy nodes, but leave a large shared-prefix subtrie where answering one query forces materializing most descendants.

- [TLE/MLE] Suffix-tree linearity disappears when edge labels, path labels, or leaf suffixes are materialized as strings instead of stored as input-index intervals. The tree can have linear nodes while the explicit labels on those edges sum to much more. Store start/end indices and materialize short labels for debugging or final output, but leave comparison, serialization, or per-edge metadata that silently copies long labels.

- [WA/RE] Ukkonen-style suffix tree code is brittle around the active point, global leaf end, edge splits, and suffix-link updates. The implementation may work when every extension creates a fresh leaf, but repeated substrings rely on exact active-node, active-edge, and active-length state. Add assertions for simple phases and rebuild tiny strings, but leave a later split where the active point lands inside an edge or a stale suffix link is followed.

- [MLE] Dense substring or pair materialization fails under large string bounds even if deduplication is expected to collapse work. Building all substrings, all prefixes per suffix, all pairwise overlaps, or all substring-pair compatibility edges is quadratic or worse on high-entropy inputs. Add length caps, interning, and early duplicate removal, but leave legal data with quadratically many genuine objects.

- [WA/TLE] Wildcard, character-class, or approximate matching becomes wrong or too slow when segments are solved independently and stitched by nearest positions. The solution assumes local LCP/LCS evidence composes into a global ordered witness. Verify short patterns and edge defects exactly, but leave repeated text where a nearest early segment blocks a later segment or two separated interior defects force many fallback checks.

- [TLE] Edit-distance or string-DP banding fails when the input model allows the optimal path to drift far from the main diagonal. It passes near-equal random pairs and intended small-edit subtasks. Widen the band near detected mismatches and exact-solve short strings, but leave early insertions or deletions so the true alignment stays outside the band or triggers a full quadratic fallback.

- [RE] Recursive trie, suffix-tree, suffix-link, or divide-and-conquer traversals can overflow the stack on a single long chain. Balanced random dictionaries hide the issue, while legal inputs may share one prefix, contain all prefixes of one string, or be one repeated character. Convert the main traversal to iterative form and special-case shallow chains, but leave deletion, cleanup, suffix-link DFS, or reporting recursion reachable at depth O(n).

- [RE] Sentinel, rotation, and substring endpoint arithmetic is fragile when empty strings, full-length matches, or boundary-touching queries are legal. The adversarial solution hardens common 1-based and 0-based cases but assumes every comparison has a next character or separator. Check short and edge substrings explicitly, but leave a rotation, suffix, wildcard segment, or separator-crossing match that reads past the sentinel or indexes -1.

- [WA] Cyclic canonicalization and rotation logic fail when multiple equal-looking anchors or phases exist. The false assumption is that the first minimum block, one Booth-style tie-break, or a gcd-derived period length determines the representative. Exact-check duplicate anchors and small cycles, but leave a highly periodic necklace or phase-shifted pair where grouping or order changes.

- [WA/TLE] Dynamic substring equality, borders, periods, and palindromes are not local under point edits. Rebuilding the edited block and neighbors passes random update streams because long global structure is rare. Keep watched periods, center windows, and periodic global rebuilds, but leave repeated strings where one edit makes distant cached structure stale or every query needs long exact repair.

- [WA/TLE] Dynamic SA/SAM/PAM shortcuts that patch only nearby ranks, states, links, or radii are wrong unless the data structure was designed for updates. A single character can change suffix order, end-position aggregation, suffix-link paths, or the longest palindromic suffix far from the edit. Rebuild on large detected changes and exact-check local windows, but leave a quiet edit sequence where no local metric crosses the rebuild threshold.

- [TLE] Dynamic string rebuilds become nonviable when operations repeatedly concatenate, split, reverse, or edit long strings and then query global properties. Weak tests use short versions or sparse edits, so rebuilding whole texts or large blocks appears acceptable. Add dirty ranges and amortized rebuild thresholds, but leave an alternating update/query sequence whose visible total edited length forces repeated full reconstruction.

- [TLE] Sqrt-decomposition or block summaries for strings fail when cross-block queries need many exact block comparisons or tails. Summaries make random intervals cheap because mismatches appear early. Cache block hashes and exact-check short ranges, but leave near-equal low-alphabet intervals where each query scans block after block before finding the decisive mismatch.

- [WA] Lazy propagation of string summaries is wrong when concatenation, reversal, case mapping, alphabet remapping, or normalization is not represented injectively. The solution assumes composed tags preserve all future equality and order predicates. Store length, endpoints, direction, and original-class metadata, but leave two different strings that share the maintained summary and later require different answers.

- [WA] Treating separator characters as impossible without enforcing that invariant breaks concatenated multi-string algorithms. The adversarial solution builds one combined text and assumes matches never cross separators or that separator order is irrelevant. Harden by adding length and source-id checks at boundaries, but leave a wildcard, normalized alphabet, or empty segment that can legally bridge across the separator model.

- [WA] Prefix/suffix DP and automaton counting with one representative per state loses future compatibility. Equal best length, count, current node, mask, or longest suffix does not capture every later overlap or extension constraint. Keep several representatives by length, endpoints, and border, plus brute force for small lengths, but leave a discarded representative or hidden forbidden-pattern overlap that is the only extendable path.

- [WA] PAM/eertree code that collapses the two roots, odd/even parity, or sentinel checks loses valid palindromes near length `0`, length `1`, and string boundaries. The suffix link of a palindrome is the longest proper palindromic suffix, so root and parity semantics are part of the state, not implementation decoration. Patch single-character and whole-string palindromes, but leave an even/odd boundary case whose suffix link should land in the other root family.

- [TLE] PAM/eertree suffix-link walks become worst-case-complexity risks when every insertion, query, or DP transition scans all palindromic suffixes under an average-sparse assumption. Online construction follows suffix links until an extendable palindrome is found, but extra per-suffix work is not free. Cache last palindromic suffixes and aggregate counts, but leave dense suffix-palindrome prefixes where the added DP or reporting layer pays for too many links.

- [WA/TLE] Eertree series-link and `diff` shortcuts fail when palindromic-factorization DP treats one leading palindrome, one parity, or one series representative as enough. Series links group suffix palindromes by length differences; omitting a member can change the best predecessor, while scanning every member can time out. Keep separate odd/even DP and exact short prefixes, but leave a long prefix where the winning factorization is hidden inside a skipped series.

- [MLE] Palindrome structures stay linear only while they store distinct palindromes or radii, not every occurrence, center range, factorization edge, or range-palindrome witness. Unary and repeated strings make the number of palindromic occurrences quadratic even though PAM and Manacher summaries are compact. Store counts or compressed intervals for dense nodes, but leave queries that need individual occurrence identities after the summary discarded them.

- [TLE] Online dictionary matching with full strings as keys becomes too slow when each operation copies or compares long strings. The problem-visible pattern is many low-alphabet keys or updates sharing long prefixes, not a container accident. Intern strings, store lengths and prefix summaries, and exact-compare only suspected ties, but leave enough tied keys that ordered maps, binary searches, or duplicate checks repeatedly scan long prefixes.

- [MLE] Persistent string structures that copy substrings, paths, rope leaves, or flattened versions on every update exceed memory even if each logical edit is small. Weak tests do not create enough versions or long shared-prefix edits. Share nodes and cap fragment materialization, but leave a version/query pattern that keeps many live roots and forces too many distinct chunks or full-string copies.

- [WA] Deleting duplicates or contained strings before solving is wrong when multiplicity, source identity, required output witnesses, or tie-breaking matter. It feels safe in superstring, dictionary, and matching tasks where containment preserves coverage. Keep counts and source ids for obvious duplicates, but leave a contained string whose separate identity affects scoring, ordering, feasibility, or reported witnesses.
